rulings.Case Title: DANIEL BOREEN VS. WELLS FARGO BANK, N.A. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion 1) Deeming Of Docs And The Truth Of Matters Specified In Requests For Admissions Admitted By Pltf; 2) Compelling Pltf To A) Respond To Form Interrogatories, Special Interrogatories; And Requests For Production; And B) Produce Documents; And 3)For Monetary Sanctions; Memo Of P&A
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, August 22, 2019, Line 1, DEFENDANT WELLS FARGO BANK, N.A. Notice Of Motion 1) Deeming Of Docs And The Truth Of Matters Specified In Requests For Admissions Admitted By Pltf; 2) Compelling Pltf To A) Respond To Form Interrogatories, Special Interrogatories; And Requests For Production; And B) Produce Documents; And 3)For Monetary Sanctions. Pro Tem Judge Scott Borrowman, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Motion to deem admitted is GRANTED. CCP 2033.280(b). Motion to compel is GRANTED. Plaintiff shall provide complete answers, without objections, no later than September 6, 2019. CCP 2030.290, 2031.300. The request for sanctions is GRANTED in the amount of $306.50 (1 hour at $246.40/hour plus $60 filing), payable by September 6, 2019. CCP 2033.280(c) (sanction is mandatory). Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to sborrowman@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: DAVID CHAPMAN VS. WATERBIRD NAVIGATION , S. A
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Defendant Mol Chemical Tankers, Pte., Ltd, To Provide Further Responses To Plaintiffs First Request For Production Of Documents
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, August 22, 2019, Line 3, PLAINTIFF DAVID CHAPMAN's Motion To Compel Defendant Mol Chemical Tankers, Pte., Ltd, To Provide Further Responses To Plaintiffs First Request For Production Of Documents. (part 1 of 2) Pro Tem Judge Scott Borrowman, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Motion GRANTED IN PART AND DENIED IN PART. California has a "prodiscovery" statutory scheme. Williams v. Sup. Ct. (2017) 3 Cal.5th 531, 540. However, the court may restrict discovery that is unduly burdensome considering "the needs of the case, the amount in controversy, and the importance of the issues at stake in the litigation." CCP 2019.030. A party moving to compel production of documents must show "good cause." CCP 2031.310(b)(1); Williams, supra, at 550. Turning to the RFPs at issue. RFP 5: Denied. There is no indication how the requested documents would be relevant to the personal injury claims or defenses. The Reply suggests that information about worldwide "roam[ing]" may be relevant but this does not indicate how such information would resolve the questions about the condition of the deck at the time of the injury. RFP 6: Granted in part. Defendant produced the portion of the survey related to the main deck, which may be sufficient. Defendant should unmask the section headings so Plaintiff can determine whether redacted sections may also be relevant. RFPs 15, 16, 20: Defendant should identify by Bates number which documents respond to which RFP. The objections based on trade secret were not asserted in the original responses and are waived. RFP 15: Denied (other than as granted regarding Bates numbers). The limitation to pilots is proportional. RFP 16: Granted. The entire Health and Safety Manual (RFP 16) appears relevant to the claims and defenses so should be produced. RFP 20. Denied (other than as granted regarding Bates numbers). The limitation to the deck is proportional. RFP 21: Granted in part. Defendant should unmask the section headings so Plaintiff can determine whether redacted sections may also be relevant. RFP 22: Denied. It is unclear how these job descriptions would be relevant to a claim or defense. RFP 26: Granted. If any documents are withheld based on privilege they must be logged. See Catalina Island Yacht Club v. Super. Ct. (2015) 242 Cal. App. 4th 1116, 1130, for the requirements in a log. RFP 34: Denied. The July 17 letter establishes privilege for the single item. (end of part 1 of 2) =(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: DONALD NORMAN VS. PATRICK STRATEMAN ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Set Aside Default And Quash Service Of Process For Lack Of Personal Jurisdiction (Part 2 Of 2 For Purposes Of Entry Of Tentative Ruling Only.)
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Thursday, August 22, 2019, Line 2, Motion To Set Aside Default And Quash Service Of Process For Lack Of Personal Jurisdiction (Part 2 Of 2 For Purposes Of Entry Of Tentative Ruling Only.) (Continued from Part 1 of 2) Defendant does not present a satisfactory excuse for not defending this action. Defendant incorrectly believed that he did not need to file a response because he is a British citizen with no connections to California. (Taaki Dec., Para. 3.) In Stiles v. Wallis (1983) 147 Cal.App.3d 1143, 1148-1149, the court denied equitable relief in similar circumstances. There, the court held that an Australian citizen who was ignorant of California law failed to establish extrinsic mistake. As in Stiles, defendant does not claim that he never received a summons that warned him of the need to respond nor does he contend that he is unfamiliar with the English language. (See also Gibble v. Car-Lene Research, Inc. (1998) 67 Cal.App.4th 295, 314-316 [trial court abused its discretion in setting aside default judgment where undisputed evidence showed defendant had actual knowledge of action and was properly served but apparently did nothing to protect its interests for more than a year, and there was no evidence of extrinsic fraud or mistake which would justify an exercise of the trial court's inherent equitable jurisdiction].) Lastly, defendant fails to demonstrate diligence. Defendant's declaration is silent regarding when defendant learned of the default. Defendant therefore fails to meet his burden to demonstrate diligence. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order that repeats the tentative ruling verbatim and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. (This is the end of the tentative ruling.)=(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: DONALD NORMAN VS. PATRICK STRATEMAN ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Set Aside Default And Quash Service Of Process For Lack Of Personal Jurisdiction
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Thursday, August 22, 2019, Line 2, DEFENDANT AMIR TAAKI'S Motion To Set Aside Default And Quash Service Of Process For Lack Of Personal Jurisdiction (Part 1 of 2 for Purposes of Entry of Tentative Ruling Only.) Defendant Taaki's motion to vacate the default entered on July 10, 2018 based upon Code of Civ. Proc., Sec. 473(b) is denied. The motion is untimely as the default was entered more than 6 months ago. "The six-month limit is mandatory; a court has no authority to grant relief under section 473, subdivision (b), unless an application is made within the six-month period." (Arumbula v. Union Carbide Corp. (2005) 128 Cal.App.4th 333, 340.) The Court has no authority to grant relief from the six-month limit, whether based on mistake, inadvertence, surprise, or excusable neglect or an attorney's affidavit of fault. (Id. at 345; accord, Austin v. Los Angeles Unified School Dist. (2016) 244 Cal.App.4th 918, 928 ["The six-month time limitation is jurisdictional; the court has no power to grant relief under section 473 once the time has lapsed."].) Defendant's motion to vacate the default based on the court's equitable powers is denied. To obtain equitable relief based on "extrinsic mistake one must satisfy three elements. First, the defaulted party must demonstrate that it has a meritorious case. Second[ ], the party seeking to set aside the default must articulate a satisfactory excuse for not presenting a defense to the original action. Last[ ], the moving party must demonstrate diligence in seeking to set aside the default once ... discovered." (Rappleyea v. Campbell (1994) 8 Cal.4th 975, 982.) "One ground for equitable relief is extrinsic mistake-a term broadly applied when circumstances extrinsic to the litigation have unfairly cost a party a hearing on the merits." (Id. at 981.) In this case, defendant fails to maintain his burden. Defendant's declaration indicates that California does not have jurisdiction over him. However, defendant did not file a motion to quash on or before the last day to plead, and did not seek a finding of good cause to file a belated motion. (Code Civ. Proc. Sec. 418.10(a).) In any event, the court cannot address the jurisdictional issue unless it finds there is good cause to vacate the default. (See Devlin v. Kearny Mesa AMC/Jeep/Renault, Inc. (1984) 155 Cal.App.3d 381, 385 ["The entry of a default terminates a defendant's rights to take any further affirmative steps in the litigation until either its default is set aside or a default judgment is entered."].) (Tentative Ruling Continues in Part 2 of 2) =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: JOSE CERON ET AL VS. SALLY LIU ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Sally Liu 1st Amended Motion To Compel
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, August 22, 2019, Line 4, DEFENDANT SALLY LIU's First Amended Motion To Compel. Pro Tem Judge Scott Borrowman, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The motion to compel responses is DENIED. Based on the Kolesnikovova Declaration, the discovery responses were timely served. Therefore, it is appropriate to evaluate whether to compel further responses. While the RFAs served to Henriquez, Rangel, Judkins, Medina were captioned Set 3, the RFAs sent to Enderson in June 2019 were captioned Set 2 and are therefore duplicative of another Set 2 sent to him in February 2019 (compare pg. 26 and pg. 99 of the PDF on the Court's online docket). Thus, the motion to compel further responses from Enderson is DENIED. CCP 2033.060. As to the other Plaintiffs, it is unclear whether the objection (asked and answered) is valid because Plaintiffs do not identify prior discovery requests that sought the specific information requested, or their responses that answered them. Therefore, the motion is GRANTED and each Plaintiff (other than Enderson) is ordered to identify where he/she answered the questions, and if not, to withdraw the objection and answer the RFA consistent with CCP 2033.220. Further responses due by September 6, 2019. Defendant's request for sanction is DENIED. There is no indication in the declaration about the basis for the requested figure such as hours worked or billable rate (unlike in the original motion) and the Court declines to award sanctions based on unsupported demands. CCP 2023.040. Plaintiffs' request for sanctions is DENIED because they are ordered to provide further responses and so are not prevailing parties. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to sborrowman@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: CHARLES E JONES III VS. SAN FRANCISCO DEPARTMENT OF PUBLIC HEALTH ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Plaintiff Deposition And Produce Documents In Deposition Notice (Continued From 8/20/19 Per Ex Parte Order File 8/9/19)
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, August 22, 2019, Line 7, Motion To Compel Plaintiff's Deposition And Produce Documents In Deposition Notice (Continued From 8/20/19 Per Ex Parte Order File 8/9/19). Pro Tem Judge Scott Borrowman, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The motion to compel is GRANTED. There is no indication Defendant "withdrew" the deposition notice as Plaintiff argues. Rather, it appears Defendant waited until now to move to compel on the original notice. The 60-day rule in CCP 2025.480 runs from "completion of the record of the deposition." Because the deposition was not completed, that clock did not start running. See Unzipped Apparel, LLC v. Bader (2007) 156 Cal.App. 4th 123 (discussing completion of "record" in subpoena context). In any event, this motion will be heard a month after Defendant requested a date for the deposition; based on the Opposition it has not yet been scheduled. This is unreasonable. Plaintiff's deposition is required to be conducted on or before August 30, 2019. CCP 2025.270 (court may shorten notice period). Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to sborrowman@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: HAROLD H. ROBINSON IV VS. JENNIFER M. JOHNSON ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION And Joinder In Similar Motion By Co-Defendant Jennifer M. Johnson
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Thursday, August 22, 2019, Line 5, DEFENDANTS SILVERBELT INVESTMENTS, LLC, AND SILVERBELT HOLDINGS, LLC'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION And Joinder In Similar Motion By Co-Defendant Jennifer M. Johnson. Plaintiff filed an amended complaint after defendants filed these motions. The court would normally take these motions off calendar as moot because the motions attack a superseded pleading. (See State Compensation Insurance Fund v. Superior Court (2010) 184 CalApp.4th 1124, 1131.) Under the particular circumstances of this case, however, the court will address the merits of these motions because defendants renewed their motions after the amended complaint was filed and plaintiff addresses the merits. (See Carlton v. Quint (2000) 77 Cal.App.4th 690, 697 ["It is well settled that the appearance of a party at the hearing of a motion and his or her opposition to the motion on its merits is a waiver of any defects or irregularities in the notice of motion. This rule applies even when no notice was given at all."].) Defendants Silverbelt Holdings, LLC and Silverbelt Investments, LLC's motion for summary adjudication is granted as to the breach of fiduciary duty cause of action. Corporations Code § 17704.09 explains that a member owes a fiduciary duty to another member. It does not provide that an entity LLC such as movants owes such a duty to its members. In fact, plaintiff's amended complaint merely alleges that defendant Johnson breached her fiduciary duty. (First Amended Complaint, Paras. 100-101.) The motion for summary adjudication is denied as to the issue of lost profits. The only claim for damages that a court can summarily adjudicate unless the ruling disposes of an entire cause of action is a claim for punitive damages. (See Code Civ. Proc., § 437c(f)(1); DeCastro West Chodorow & Burns, Inc. v. Superior Court (1996) 47 Cal.App.4th 410, 421-422.) Therefore, this aspect of the motion is procedurally infirm, as defendants' reply concedes. The motion for summary adjudication is granted as to the claim for unjust enrichment. A quasi contract claim does not lie where the parties have an express contract that governs their relationship. (See California Medical Association, Inc. v. Aetna US Healthcare of California, Inc. (2001) 94 Cal.App.4th 151, 172.) Plaintiff is correct that he can plead quantum meruit and breach of contract as alternative theories of relief. (See Newport Harbor Ventures, LLC v. Morris Cerullo World Evangelism (2016) 6 Cal.App.5th 1207, 1223.) In this case, however, defendants concede that the contracts are enforceable. (Reply, 8:19-27 ["Defendants have never contested the validity or enforceability of those contracts."].) Plaintiff thus has no further basis to pursue the claim. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for movants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats the tentative ruling verbatim and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: HAROLD H. ROBINSON IV VS. JENNIFER M. JOHNSON ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Thursday, August 22, 2019, Line 5, DEFENDANT JENNIFER JOHNSON'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY ADJUDICATION. Plaintiff filed an amended complaint after defendants filed these motions. The court would normally take these motions off calendar as moot because the motions attack a superseded pleading. (See State Compensation Insurance Fund v. Superior Court (2010) 184 CalApp.4th 1124, 1131.) Under the particular circumstances of this case, however, the court will address the merits of these motions because defendants renewed their motions after the amended complaint was filed and plaintiff addresses the merits. (See Carlton v. Quint (2000) 77 Cal.App.4th 690, 697 ["It is well settled that the appearance of a party at the hearing of a motion and his or her opposition to the motion on its merits is a waiver of any defects or irregularities in the notice of motion. This rule applies even when no notice was given at all."].) Defendant Johnson's motion for summary adjudication is denied as to the breach of fiduciary duty cause of action. Defendant Johnson fails to maintain her burden of production. The only issue that the separate statement attempts to establish is that the parties did not have a "partnership." (UMF 1-3.) Defendant fails to show that she did not breach her fiduciary duty as a manager member of the LLCs. "The manager of an LLC has a fiduciary duty and owes to the members of the LLC the same duties of loyalty and good faith as a partner owes to the partnership and its partners." (Feresi v. The Livery, LLC (2014) 232 Cal.App.4th 419, 425; see also Corps. Code § 17704.09(d), (f)(2).) The motion for summary adjudication is denied as to the issue of lost profits. The only claim for damages that a court can summarily adjudicate unless the ruling disposes of an entire cause of action is a claim for punitive damages. (See Code Civ. Proc., § 437c(f)(1); DeCastro West Chodorow & Burns, Inc. v. Superior Court (1996) 47 Cal.App.4th 410, 421-422.) Therefore, this aspect of the motion is procedurally infirm, as defendants' reply concedes. The motion for summary adjudication is granted as to the claim for unjust enrichment. A quasi contract claim does not lie where the parties have an express contract that governs their relationship. (See California Medical Association, Inc. v. Aetna US Healthcare of California, Inc. (2001) 94 Cal.App.4th 151, 172.) Plaintiff is correct that he can plead quantum meruit and breach of contract as alternative theories of relief. (See Newport Harbor Ventures, LLC v. Morris Cerullo World Evangelism (2016) 6 Cal.App.5th1207, 1223.) In this case, however, defendants concede that the contracts are enforceable. (Reply, 8:19-27 ["Defendants have never contested the validity or enforceability of those contracts."].) Plaintiff thus has no further basis to pursue the claim. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for movant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats the tentative ruling verbatim and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: JENNIFER COLIN VS. THYSSENKRUPP ELEVATOR COMPANY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Quash Subpoenas; Request For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, August 22, 2019, Line 5, PLAINTIFF JENNIFER COLIN's Motion To Quash Subpoenas; Request For Sanctions. Pro Tem Judge Scott Borrowman, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: The motion to quash is DENIED. Thyssenkrupp Elevator's meet-and-confer offer to permit Plaintiff a first look at the records and to designate items under the existing stipulated protective order is reasonably protective of Plaintiff's privacy interests. The Court adopts the proposal as an order. Defendant's request for sanctions is DENIED - while it is a close call, the Court concludes that Plaintiff's advocacy to limit disclosure of private medical information is substantial justification for purposes of CCP 2025.410(d). Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to sborrowman@gmail.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/JPT)

rulings.Case Title: ** TRANSFERRED TO RIVERSIDE CO.** PHILIP JOHN STUTO ET AL VS. GE HEALTHCARE, INC. ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendants Ge Healthcare Inc. And General Electric Company'S Notice Of Motion And Motion To Transfer; Supporting Memorandum Of Points And Authorities
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Thursday, August 22, 2019, Line 7, DEFENDANTS GE HEALTHCARE, INC. AND GENERAL ELECTRIC COMPANY'S Motion To Transfer. Defendants GE Healthcare Inc. And General Electric Company's motion to transfer venue to Riverside County Superior Court pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure Sec. 397(c) is denied. Defendants have submitted no supporting declarations or affidavits and have failed to meet their burden to show that transfer is warranted to promote the convenience of witness and the ends of justice. (Lieppman v. Lieber (1986) 180 Cal.App.3d 914, 919 ["'The burden rests upon one who seeks a change of venue under [Code of Civil Procedure, section 397, subdivision 3] to prove that both the convenience of witnesses and ends of justice will be promoted thereby, and this he must do through affidavits that contain more than generalities and conclusions.'"]; Stute v. Burinda (1981) 123 Cal.App.3d Supp. 11, 17 ["When a motion for change of venue is made under Code of Civil Procedure, section 397, subdivision 3, on the grounds that the convenience of witnesses and the ends of justice would be promoted by the changes, the supporting declarations must show (1) the names of each witness, (2) the expected testimony of each witness, and (3) facts showing why the attendance of said witnesses at trial will be inconvenient and why the ends of justice would be served by a transfer"]); Flanagan v. Flanagan (1959) 175 Cal.App.2d 641, 645-646 [the affidavit must aver facts from which the court to make a finding as to the convenience of witness and to conclude that the end of justice would be served by the venue change].) Defendants' sole reliance on plaintiffs' allegations in the unverified complaint is misplaced. "Such a motion must, of course, be supported by competent evidence." (Tutor-Saliba-Perini Joint Venture v. Superior Court (1991) 233 Cal.App.3d 736, 744.) rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Thursday, August 22, 2019, Line 9, DEFENDANT STATE OF CALIFORNIA DEPARTMENT OF TRANSPORTATION'S AMENDED DEMURRER. Plaintiff Peninsula Corridor Joint Powers Board filed its first amended complaint (FAC) on August 8, 2019. (See State Compensation Insurance Fund v. Superior Court (2010) 184 CalApp.4th 1124, 1131 ["the filing of an amended complaint moots a motion directed to a prior complaint."].) If, as the State indicates in its supplemental declaration, it does not believe the amendments overcome the defects in the prior complaint, it must meet and confer again with Plaintiff's counsel and then, if unable to resolve the issues, file a new demurrer. (See Code Civ. Proc. Sec. 430.41 ["If an amended complaint . . . is filed, the responding party shall meet and confer again with the party who filed the amended pleading before filing a demurrer to the amended pleading."].) rulings.Case Title: MARIA DEL CARMEN GONZALEZ VS. LAURENT LEGENDRE ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Petition(Motion) For Order Compelling Arbitration: Notice Of Hearing Thereon
rulings.Rulings: Matter on the Law & Motion Calendar for Thursday, August 22, 2019, Line 11, PLAINTIFF MARIA GONZALEZ'S Motion For Order Compelling Arbitration. Plaintiff Maria Del Carmen Gonzalez's motion to compel arbitration, in which defendant Macavini, Inc. joined, is denied. The general rule is that non-parties to an arbitration agreement may not be compelled to arbitrate disputes under the agreement. (See Suh v. Superior Court (2010) 181 Cal.App.4th 1504, 1512 ["Even the strong public policy in favor of arbitration does not extend to those who are not parties to an arbitration agreement or who have not authorized anyone to act for them in executing such an agreement."].) While there are a number of theories that serve as exceptions to this general rule (see id. at 1513), plaintiff has failed to show that any of those exceptions applies to the broker defendants. A nonsignatory can be compelled to arbitrate on the basis of an agency relation between the signatory and the nonsignatory when the relationship "makes it equitable to impose the duty to arbitrate upon the non-signatory." (Westra v. Marcus & Millichap Real Estate Investment Brokerage Co., Inc. (2005) 129 Cal.App.4th 759, 765.) However, a showing of agency relationship does not suffice; rather, the court must also examine "whether that preexisting relationship is of such a nature that it supports a finding of 'implied authority for [one of the signatories] to bind [the nonsignatory] by their arbitration agreement." (Jensen v. U-Haul Co. of California (2017) 18 Cal.App.5th 295, 303.) It may be equitable for "a signatory to compel a nonsignatory to arbitrate so long as another signatory had authority to bind the nonsignatory to the arbitration agreement. [Citation.] The requirements for imposing arbitration on a nonsignatory principal, as opposed to allowing a nonsignatory agent to compel arbitration, however, must be 'exacting.'" (Cohen v. TNP 2008 Participating Notes Program, LLC (2019) 31 Cal.App.5th 840, 864.) Here, unlike in Westra v. Marcus & Millichap Real Estate Investment Brokerage Co., Inc. (2005) 129 Cal.App.4th 759, plaintiff and defendant are seeking to compel the nonsignatory broker defendants to arbitrate. "It is one thing to permit a nonsignatory to relinquish his right to a jury trial, but quite another to compel him to do so." (Benasra v. Marciano (2001) 92 Cal.App.4th 987, 991.) Most importantly, the language of the agreement explicitly states that "Brokers are not Parties to this Agreement"; provides that the Parties "agree to mediate any disputes or claims with Broker(s) who, in writing, agree to such mediation prior to, or within a reasonable time after, the dispute or claim is presented to the Broker"; and, critically, that "[B]rokers shall not be obligated or compelled to mediate or arbitrate unless they agree so in writing." (Agreement Paras. 1(F), 30 (B) and (C) (3).) Thus, the agreement expressly contradicts the notion that the brokers may be compelled to arbitrate claims against them unless they agree to do so in writing, whether on an agency or other theory. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the broker defendants is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats the tentative ruling verbatim and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)
